question: What tool brands are good for basic home repairs and small DIY projects?
answer_text:
For basic home repairs and small DIY projects, you don’t need the most expensive pro-grade tools. Here are reliable brands that offer a great balance of quality, affordability, and durability for homeowners:

Top Tier (Still Affordable for DIY)

Ryobi – The go-to for cordless power tools. Huge selection, frequent sales, and batteries work across all their tools. Perfect for occasional use.

Black+Decker – Great for very light duty and simple repairs. Excellent entry-level price, especially for corded drills, screwdrivers, and sanders.

Mid-Range (Excellent Value)

Craftsman – A classic DIY brand. Hand tools have a lifetime warranty. Power tools are solid for weekend projects. Widely available at Lowe’s and Ace.

Stanley – Best known for hand tools (hammers, screwdrivers, tape measures, levels). Indestructible for home use.

Husky (Home Depot) – Hand tools only, but lifetime warranty and very affordable. Great for wrenches, pliers, and tool sets.

Budget-Friendly (Still Reliable)

Hyper Tough (Walmart) – Surprisingly decent for very occasional use (e.g., hanging pictures, assembling furniture). Not for heavy work.

Harbor Freight – Pittsburgh (hand tools) – Fine for basic repairs. Lifetime warranty on hand tools. Avoid their cheapest power tools.

Hand Tools Only (Best-in-Class for DIY)

Klein Tools – For electrical repairs (wire strippers, voltage testers, screwdrivers). Worth the small premium.

Channellock – Pliers and wrenches. Bulletproof.

What to Avoid for Basic DIY?
Very cheap no-name brands from Amazon or dollar stores (poor fit, break easily). Also, heavy pro brands like Hilti or Snap-on are overkill.

Bottom line: Start with Ryobi for power tools + Stanley or Husky for hand tools. You’ll be set for 95% of home repairs and small projects without overspending.
